reasoning: The Lean statement asserts exactly the informal claim. It takes a `NormalSpace X`, arbitrary disjoint closed sets `A`, `B`, and produces open sets `U`, `V` containing `A` and `B` respectively with `closure U ∩ closure V = ∅`. An open set containing a set is precisely a neighborhood of that set (and conversely, any neighborhood contains an open neighborhood with smaller-or-equal closure), so "neighborhoods whose closures are disjoint" matches the existence of such open `U`, `V`. Disjointness of the closures is encoded as `closure U ∩ closure V = ∅`, which for sets is equivalent to `Disjoint`. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the quantifier structure (∀ disjoint closed A, B; ∃ neighborhoods) is preserved.
